f14325429dbe.title: Flora Rustica
f14325429dbe.subtitle: Exhibiting accurate figures of such plants as are either useful or injurious in husbandry.
f14325429dbe.description:
*Flora Rustica* is an illustrated botanical work by Thomas Martyn, published in London by Frederick P. Nodder beginning in 1792. Its full title describes its purpose clearly: to present “accurate figures” of plants that are either useful or harmful in husbandry. The work combines scientific classification, popular description, and practical observations, with illustrations drawn and engraved by Frederick P. Nodder.

The book is concerned less with ornamental botany than with the practical relationship between plants and agriculture. Martyn describes species according to their appearance, properties, range, and agricultural significance, helping readers identify plants that could benefit farming, grazing, medicine, or rural household use, as well as plants considered troublesome weeds or otherwise injurious.

As a late eighteenth-century agricultural flora, *Flora Rustica* reflects the period’s effort to connect Linnaean botanical science with everyday rural practice. Its value lies in its combination of precise plant illustration, taxonomic description, and applied commentary for landowners, farmers, gardeners, and naturalists. Modern scholarship has noted its importance as an early illustrated guide to plants in British agricultural systems and as a precursor to later weed-identification manuals.
f14325429dbe.notes: Source: Internet Archive — Flora rustica: exhibiting accurate figures of such plants as are either useful or injurious in husbandry. Drawn and engraved by Frederick P. Nodder, ... and coloured under his inspection. With scientific characters, popular descriptions, and useful observations, by Thomas Martyn, ... 1792: Vol 2
